SYNTHETIC NOTE. Not a real patient. For testing only.

Springfield Orthopedic & Spine Associates
Pain Medicine Follow-Up Visit
Patient: Sample, Robert (SYNTHETIC)      MRN: SYN000456
DOB: 03/15/1968                          Sex: M
Encounter Date: 06/01/2026               Provider: A. Clinician, MD

Chief Complaint:
Persistent left leg radicular pain despite conservative care; here to
discuss epidural steroid injection.

HPI:
Mr. Sample is a 58 y/o man with 10 weeks of low back pain radiating down
the left posterior leg to the ankle in an L5 distribution. Pain is rated
7/10 at worst, interferes with sleep, and limits standing to about 20
minutes and walking to about two blocks. He denies saddle anesthesia,
bowel or bladder dysfunction, fever, or weight loss.

Conservative Care to Date:
Completed 8 weeks of physical therapy with only transient relief.
Naproxen and a short course of oral steroids provided partial, temporary
improvement. No prior epidural steroid injection.

Imaging:
MRI lumbar spine (05/10/2026): Left paracentral disc protrusion at L4-L5
contacting the traversing left L5 nerve root, with mild left lateral
recess narrowing. No high-grade stenosis.

Physical Exam:
Positive straight leg raise on the left at 45 degrees reproducing leg
pain. Diminished sensation over the left L5 dermatome. Strength 4+/5 left
extensor hallucis longus. Reflexes symmetric. No long tract signs.

Assessment:
Left L5 lumbar radiculopathy secondary to L4-L5 disc protrusion seen on
MRI, persistent despite 8 weeks of conservative therapy including physical
therapy and pharmacologic management.

Plan:
Recommend left L4-L5 transforaminal epidural steroid injection. Baseline
pain 7/10 with standing tolerance of 20 minutes and walking tolerance of
two blocks documented for response tracking. No active infection and no
anticoagulation; no contraindication identified. Risks, benefits, and
alternatives discussed; patient wishes to proceed.

Electronically signed by A. Clinician, MD on 06/01/2026.
